parties: Eurosemillas S.A. v CPVO, Nadorcott S.A.R.L., Carpa Dorada S.L.
title: CPVO BoA A002/2020 - 'Nadorcott'
text:
Appeal Case A002/2020 
DECISION 
of the Board of Appeal of 2 January 2023  
 
 
Concerning the appeal lodged by  Eurosemillas, SA  Paseo de la Victoria, 31, 14004 Córdoba (Spain) Represented by Mr. J. Muñoz-Delgado y Mérida  
Paseo de la Castellana, nº 216, 28046 Madrid (Spain)   Appellant 
Other parties to the appeal procedure:   Nadorcott Protection S.A.R.L.  
51 rue Jules Barbier, Le Stanisla s, 83700 Saint-Raphaël (France)  
Represented by Ms. I. Pi i Amorós  
Príncipe de Vergara, 187, Plaza de Rodrigo Uría, 28002 Madrid (Spain) 
  Title holder 
and  Carpa Dorada, S.L.  C/Manuel Vivanco, 12, 12005 Almazora (Spain) Represented by Ms. I. Pérez-Cabrero-Fernández Plaza del Ayuntamiento, 29, 46002 Valencia (Spain)  Intervener 
 vs.  Community Plant Variety Office 3 boulevard Maréchal Foch, CS 10121,49101 Angers Cedes 2 (France) Represented b y Ms. M. García-Moncó Fuente 
 Defendant                                    
 
 
 
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right No  EU 14111 
Variety denomination: Nadorcott  
Species: 
Citrus reticulata Blanco  
 
On 2 January 2023 the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed of P. van der Kooij 
(Chairman), S. Martin (Rapporteur) and H. Ghijsen (Member), gave the following decision: 
1. The appeal is admissible but not well-founded and is therefore dismissed.  
2. Decision No. NN 20 is upheld. 
3. The costs of the appeal proceedings shall be borne by the Appellant, pursuant to Article 
85 (1) of Council Regulation No. 2100/94. 

  
 2 / 18
I.      SUMMARY OF THE FACTS  
a. The development of the variety ‘Nadorcott’ 
1. In 1963-64 trees of the ‘Murcott’ variety were planted in several plots of the Institut National de la Recherche 
Agronomique (INRA) of Morocco, among them one in the town of Afourer.  
2. In 1981-82, the INRA researcher, Mr. Nadori, found in th e seedling population originating from the ‘Murcott’ 
variety, planted in 1963/64, the original tree of the new variety encoded as INRA 21 W. This tree, apparently a cross between ‘Murcott’ and an unknown pollinizer
, drew his attention since its fruit showed a better 
coloration, easy peeling and less seeds, all characteristics highly appreciated for its commercial interest. 
 
3. Between 1982 and 1983, INRA 21 W grafts were sent to Société des Services Agricoles au Maroc (SASMA) – 
a body that included farmers in the citrus sector in Morocco- under the name INRA W in order to perform behaviour tests. Accordingly, between 1983-84, SASMA distributed buds of INRA 21 W to farmers in different regions to observe their behaviour. But the experiment was not successful: the trees produced fruit with an 
excessive number of seeds so the variety was not considered to be of interest and therefore the trees
 were 
removed by the farmers in 1985. 
4. In 1988, Mr. Nadori identified on the Domaines Agrico les estate of Dar Es Salam five-years old INRA W 
trees initially planted under the name ‘Murcott SASMA’ that were later re-named ‘Afourer’ at his request 
to make allusion to its origin and to av oid confusion with the variety ‘Murcott’.  
5. During the spring of 1988 Mr Nadori noticed that while some green-yellowish fruits of those trees (a result 
of out-of-season flowering) did not have seeds, those with an orange-pink tone had many.  
6. In 1989, Mr Nadori covered ‘Afourer’ trees from Dar Es  Salam together with the INRA 21 W tree at Afourer’s 
INRA testing station with an anti-insect net before  the flowering period. The trees covered produced 
seedless fruits while the uncovered trees had many se eds. Mr. Nadori thus proved that the INRA 21 W - 
Afourer variety is self-incompatible. 
7. In September 1989, Mr. Nadori informed both Domaines  Agricoles and INRA of his discovery. He also 
informed INRA of the need to start experimental pl ots for an assessment of the ‘Afourer’ variety on the 
market. 
8. Based on such request, between 1990 and 1991, an ex perimental plot was carried out in Morocco, in a 
locality without citrus trees. In said  plot, it was found that the new va riety had no seeds only when cross-
pollination was prevented. New experiment al plots were subsequently developed. 
b. Filing of an application for registration of Community plant variety rights for the 
variety ‘Nadorcott’ 
9. On 22 August 1995, Mr Jean De Maistre submitted to the Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter also 
referred to as ‘the Office’) an app lication for registration of Community plant variety rights for the variety 
‘Nadorcott’ of the botanical taxon Citrus reti culata Blanco (Application number 1995/0726).   
10. On 21 March 1997, Mr. De Maistre informed the Office of the transfer of all his rights concerning the 
‘Nadorcott’ variety to Nadorco tt Protection S.A.R.L. (NCP) , its current owner.  
11. On 4 October 2004, the Office granted the Community Plant Variety Right (CPVR) for the plant variety 
‘Nadorcott’ (Registration No. EU 14111).   
12. The registration of the grant was published in the Official Journal of the Office of 15 December 2004. 
c. The first application for annulment  
13. On 11 February 2005, the Valencian Federation of Agri cultural Cooperatives (FECOAV) lodged an appeal 
against that decision. 
14. The application was rejected by a decision of the Board of Appeal of 8 November 2005 (Decision 
A001/2005).  
15. The decision was confirmed by the Genera l Court (31/01/2008, T- 95/06, EU:T:2008:25).  
 

  
 3 / 18
d. The second application for annulment  
16. On 31 May 2016, the Office received an application fo r annulment of the CPVR for ‘Nadorcott’ (hereinafter 
referred to as the “Request for annulment”) by Eurosemi llas SA (hereinafter referred to as ‘the Appellant’ 
or ‘Eurosemillas/Euroseeds’). 
17. The Request for annulment is based on Article 20(1 )(a) and (c) of Council Regulation (EC) No. 2100/94 of 
27 July 1994 on Community plant variety rights (hereinaft er also referred to as the ‘Basic Regulation’ or 
‘BR’). 
18. Essentially, the Appellant alleges a lack  of entitlement and a lack of nove lty of the ‘Nadorcott’ plant variety 
resulting from its commercialisation, albeit under the denomination ‘Afourer’, prior to 1980 in the territory 
of the European Union.   
19. On 6 June 2016, the Request for annulment was transmi tted by the Office to the owner of the plant variety 
‘Nadorcott’. 
20. On 2 August 2016 the Office received a request from Ca rpa Dorada, SL (hereinafter ‘the interested party’ 
or ‘Carpa Dorada’) to be considered  a party to the nullity proceedings.  
21. The request was granted on 5 August 2016.  
22. On 14 December 2016, the owner and the interested pa rty filed their observations and comments to the 
Request for annulment.  
23. On 8 March 2017, the Appellant forma lly requested the Office, on the basi s of the right to be heard under 
Article 56(2) Proceedings Regulation No 874/2009 (‘Implemen ting Rules’ or ‘PR’), to be given three months 
to submit observations in response to the pleadings of the other two parties.  
24. On 13 March 2027, the Office granted said request.  
25. On 9 June 2017, the Office received the response from the Appellant to the observations and comments 
filed by the owner and the interested party (hereinafter referred to as the ‘responses’).  
26. On 20 March 2018, the Office received the observatio ns of the owner and the interested party to the 
responses.  
e. The contested decision  
27. On 16 December 2019, the Office issued Decision  No. NN 20 rejecting the request for annulment 
(hereinafter referred to as the ‘Contested decision’). 
f. The present proceedings 
28. On 15 January 2020 the Appellant filed an appeal agai nst the Contested decision, followed on 21 April 
2020 by its Statement of grounds (hereinafter also the ‘SOG’).   
29. On 15 December 2020, the Office filed its observat ions in reply to the statement of grounds.  
g. The hearing 
30. The oral hearing took place on 3 October 2022 in Ange rs, France. The hearing was attended by all parties 
to the proceedings duly represented.  
 
II.     REASONS 
On the admissibility of the appeal 
31. The appeal complies with Articles 67, 68 and 69 BR. It is therefore admissible. 
  

  
 4 / 18
On the substance 
1. Preliminary remarks  
32. The Appellant raises nine grounds of appeal whic h will be addressed in the order proposed by the 
Appellant. 
33. However, before entering into the examination of the merits of the present appeal, the Board recalls that 
the Office enjoys “a wide discretion concerning an nulment of a plant variety right for the purposes of 
Article 20 of Regulation No 2100/94, in so far as th e protected variety underwent the examination set out 
in the previous paragraph” (21 May 2015, C- 546/12P, Lemon Symphony II, EU:C:2015:332, § 56).  
34. As a result it is “only where there are serious doubts th at the conditions laid down in Articles 7 or 10 of 
that regulation had been met on th e date of the examination provided for under Articles 54 and 55 of that 
regulation can a re-examination of the protected vari ety by way of nullity proceedings under Article 20 of 
Regulation No 2100/94 be justified” ((21 May 2015,  C-546/12P,  Lemon Symphony II, EU:C:2015:332, § 
56).  
35. As outlined by the Court of Justice, the burden of pr oof lies with the moving party, the Appellant in the 
present case: “In that context, a third party seekin g annulment of a plant variety right must adduce 
evidence and facts of suffici ent substance to raise serious doubts as to the legality of the grant of a plant 
variety right following the examination provided for in  Articles 54 and 55 of that regulation (21 May 2015, 
C-546/12P,  Lemon Symphony II, EU:C:2015:332, § 57).  
36. This principle is now enshrined in article 53(a) PR  as regards proceedings before the Community Plant 
Variety Office which provides that “2.  A request to  the Office to open the proceedings on nullity or 
cancellation, as referred to in Articles 20 and 21, resp ectively, of the basic regula tion, shall be accompanied 
by evidence and facts raising seri ous doubts as to the validity”.  
37. This being said, the Board notices that the ‘Nadorcott’  variety which is the object of the present appeal 
has already been examined by the Office on three diffe rent occasions: (1) in the course of the examination 
procedure; (2) in the context of the appeal filed by the Federation of Agricultural Cooperatives of the 
Valencian Community (FECOAV) on 18 April 2005; (3) in  the course of the present annulment procedure. 
Following these in-depth and complex examinations, the Office confirmed the validity of the plant variety 
right granted to ‘Nadorcott’.    
 
2. First ground of appeal: The alleged Violation of the fundamental right to good administration, 
effective judicial protection and a the right to a fair trial resulting from the alleged breach by 
the Office of its duty to investigate facts on its own motion in the course of the invalidity 
proceedings  
38. The Appellant submits that the Office failed (1) to properly investigate the matter in the course of the 
examination proceedings and subsequently in the cour se of the invalidity proceedings; (2) to request the 
appearance of witnesses as proposed by the Appellant ; (3) to commission an expert report; (4) to order 
the production of relevant documents in the hand of the parties or third parties.  
39. Before entering into the meri ts of this first claim, it is useful to recall the relevant principles regarding the 
burden of proof in invalidity proceedings. 
2.1  Burden of proof in invalidity proceedings 
40. In invalidity proceedings the burden  of proof lies with the requesting party. That obligation stems from 
Article 53a PR and the case-law of the Genera l Court and the Court of Justice (21 May 2015, 
Schräder/OCVV, C ‑546/12 P, EU:C:2015:332, §57; 23 November 2017, M 02205, T ‑140/15, 
EU:T:2017:830, § 58) 
41. The Appellant submits that its burden of proof is lim ited to prima facie evidence that there are serious 
doubts about the validity of the Community protection . Once the nullity application is admitted the burden 
of proof would shift to the Office who would be requ ired to carry out an inve stigation of the facts and 
evidence to the full satisfaction of the a pplicant, pursuant to Articles 76 and 60 BR.  
42. The Board does not share that positi on. The initial admission of an appl ication for nullity means that the 
Office is satisfied that prima facie there are grounds for examining in detail the materials submitted in the 

  
 5 / 18
nullity application and to initiate inter partes  proceedings. But it does not im ply that the burden of proof 
shifts towards the Office and the owner. It also does  not imply that the Office is required to conduct 
investigations pursuant to the wishes and desires of the Appellant.  
2.2 The right of the Office to conduct measures  of instruction and investigations must be 
reconciled with the principle of “equality of arms” 
43. The Board emphasizes that Invalidi ty proceedings are by definition inter partes  proceedings in the course 
of which the Office shall remain impart ial in accordance with the principle of equality of arms which is part 
of EU Law (19 May 2021, T-510/19, DEVICE OF A Jumping ANIMAL (fig.)/Puma (fig.) et al., 
EU:T:2021:281, § 70).  
44. In invalidity proceedings the burden  of proof lies with the parties (21 May 2015, Schräder/OCVV, C ‑546/12 
P, EU:C:2015:332, §57; 23 November 2017, M 02205, T ‑140/15, EU:T:2017:830, § 58). In view of its 
obligation to remain impartial the Office shall not conduct investigations of facts on its own volition.  
45. The Board acknowledges that it is bound by the princi ple of sound administration, which requires it to 
examine carefully and impartially all th e relevant facts of a case and to obtain all the elements of law and 
of fact necessary for the exercise of its discretion (19 December 2012,  Brookfield New Zealand and Elaris 
v CPVO and Schniga, C-534/10 P, EU: C: 2012: 813, pa r. 51;  8 June 2017, Schniga  v CPVO, C-625/15  
P, EU: C: 2017: 435, par. 47; 11 April 2019,  Kiku GmbH, T-765/17, EU: T: 2019: 244, par. 41; 23 
November 2017,  T-140/15, Aurora/OCVV-SVanderhave (M 02205),  EU: T: 2017: 830, par. 73).  
46. Article 76 BR is an expression of the duty of sound administration, enshrined in Article 41 of the Charter 
of Fundamental Rights, which entail s the duty of the competent inst itution to examine carefully and 
impartially all the relevant aspects of  the individual case. It also requir es the Board of Appeal to examine 
carefully and impartially all the rele vant facts and law of the case provided that they have been adduced 
by the parties (11 April 2019, T-765/ 17, Kiku GmbH, EU:T:2019:244, §32). 
47. It does not imply that the Office is required to initia te investigations to clarif y and possibly to support the 
arguments raised by one party. In that regard, it is apparent from the case-law that the Office cannot be 
required to carry out further investigations where it considers that the preliminary investigation of the case 
has been sufficient (Case 9/83 Eisen und Metall v Commission [1984] ECR 2071, paragraph 32, and Case 
T‑141/94 Thyssen Stahl v Commission [1999] ECR II ‑347, paragraph 110). 
48. In the present case, the Office, after analysing the su bmissions and evidence of all parties, came to the 
conclusion that it had gathered all necessary eviden ce. It was therefore entitled to reject the various 
requests presented by the Appellant. This decision does not amount to arbitr ariness (18 September 2012, 
T-133/08, Schräder §§136-141).  
2.3  The alleged failure of the Office to investigate the matter 
49. The Appellant declares in its application that it ha s “no more evidence to present” but that relevant 
information and documents are in the possession and under the control of NCP and under the exclusive control of its members, the IN RA and Domaines Agricoles.  
50. The Appellant therefore asked the Office to conduct an investigation and to request the production of 
documents regarding the following issues: 
1. The identity of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety with the ‘Afourer’ variety. 
2. The timing, the circumstances and the persons and entities that actually took part in the alleged 
discovery and development of the mandarin  variety INRA 21 W — Murcott/Afourer. 
3. The nature, extent and dates of the different acts of disposal of components of the variety carried out 
by Mr Nadori prior to the relevant grace period; 
(a) The cultivation in Morocco and the export to the European Union of fr uit of the mandarin variety, then 
known as ‘Afourer’, prior to its alleged discov ery by Mr Nadori at INRA’s premises in 1981. 
(b) The transfer of propagating material from ‘Afour er’ to Moroccan producers, and in particular to the 
Domaines Agricoles, carried out in 1982/83 by INRA, directly or via the SASMA nurseries. 
(c) The marketing in Morocco of fruit of the harvest of plantations carried out by those Moroccan producers 
on the basis of the propagating material referred to in the previous paragraph. 

  
 6 / 18
(d) The use by Domaines Agricoles of the propagating material in Morocco, the ma rketing of the fruit of 
the variety in Morocco, the ma rketing of the fruit of the harvest, th e conservation of at least 7 of those 
trees in the Dar Es Salam plant, the use of propag ating material obtained from these trees for the 
production of village plantations in  different locations, particularly planting 200 hectares in Agafay and 
exporting the fruits of the harvest of those plantations to the European  Union at the time of October 1988 
or 22 August 1994. 
(e) The transfer of propagating material from ‘Afourer ’ to UCR (University of California Riverside, a US 
Institute of research) carried out in 1984 and 1985 by Mr Nadori with the authorisation of INRA, its 
subsequent addition to the Collection of UCR; its rele ase into California nurseries and its bringing into 
production and marketing by the growers in that State. 
(f) The transfer of propagating material from ‘Afourer ’ to the Spanish producer, Mr Boronat Flores, carried 
out by Domaines Agricoles in 1988, its subsequent su pply of propagating material to Spanish growers and 
the production by those plantations of ‘Afourer’ and marketing of the fruits of the harvest in Spain from 
1990 onwards. 
(g) The exportation by Moroccan producers to the European Union of ‘Afourer’ fruit prior to 22 August 
1994. 
4. The circumstances relating to the negotiation an d content of the agreements possibly reached between 
Mr Nadori and Mr De Maistre and Domaines Agricoles an d the INRA of Morocco in relation to the right to 
obtaining the Community protection of Nadorcott in accordance with the rules of procedure Basis and 
applicable legislation of Morocco, its share of NC P ownership and the commercial exploitation of the 
Nadorcott variety” (SOG, par. 32).  
51. It is undisputed that the Office did not order any meas ure of instruction and relied  solely on the evidence 
adduced by the Parties. 
52. The Office justified its position on the basis that a ll these issues had been already investigated in the 
course of the examination proceedings:  
“However, it is not apparent in the present invalidity proceedings that it is necessary to investigate of its 
own motion [any] other fact, given, moreover, that this  is the third occasion on which the Office examines 
and it appreciates the ‘Nadorcott’ variety and the vali dity of its title.” (conte sted decision, § 217-218).  
53. The Board recalls that it is trite law that a party must  present prima facie evidence in order to secure the 
adoption of a measure of enquiry (21 May 2015, C-546/12P, Lemon Symphony II, EU:C:2015:332, § 76).  
54. The impartial examination of the facts and documentary evidence submitted by the Appellant did not 
prompt any doubts about the validity of the Comm unity protection of the plant variety ‘Nadorcott’. 
55. This is the very reason why the Appellant asked the Office to conduct further investigation measures in 
case it would reveal further grounds of invalidity. Th is request amounts to nothing more than a "fishing 
expedition”. 
56. Moreover, the Appellant did not advance any evidence whatsoever, or the slightest information to justify 
his request. Indeed, it is not sufficient to contes t the truthfulness of the allegations made by the 
respondent and the interveners. The Appellant should ha ve adduced evidence likely to contradict the facts 
and documents put forward by the owner.  
57. For instance, the Appellant could have contacted direct ly Prof Bitters, or a representative of Domaines 
Agricoles, Mr Ouammou Mohamed and Mr Ouaïcha Rahh ou (the alleged codeveloper of the ‘Nadorcott’ 
variety). It chose not to do so. In stead, the Appellant would have preferred that the Office conduct such 
investigations.   
58. Finally, once again the Board recalls that if the Office  finds that it already has the evidence necessary to 
reach a decision, the Office cannot be required to carr y out additional investigatio ns, particularly if they 
are disproportionate or could lead to a delay in the procedure. (Case 9/83 Eisen und Metall v Commission 
[1984] ECR 2071, § 32, and Case T ‑141/94 Thyssen Stahl v Commission [1999] ECR II ‑347, § 110). 
59. En passant , the Board notices the plant variety protection was granted after a lengthy procedure (followed 
by an appeal by the Federation of Agricultural Cooperatives of the Valencian Community (FECOAV)), in which the CPVO had already examined the vast majority  of the factual and legal elements provided by the 
Appellant.  

  
 7 / 18
60. Consequently, there has been no breach of the principl e of good administration or of the duty to examine 
the facts of its own motion.  
2.4  The Appellant’s request to summon witnesses  
61. The Appellant asked the Office to compel  the appearance of eight witnesses.  
62. The request was dismissed since “the Office has not fo und contradictions requiring further clarification by 
the witnesses referred to by the applicant, so that th ere is no need for further investigation’ (Contested 
decision § 223).  
63. The Appellant could have contacted the proposed witne sses with the view of signing a witness statement.  
64. During the hearing in response to a question from th e Board, the Appellant confirmed that it had not tried 
to get in touch with any of the witnesses.   
65. The Appellant could have secured the testimony of th e proposed witnesses but it chose not to do so. 
2.5  For the insufficient evidence of the origin of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety, the Appellant requests 
to appoint an “independent” expert 
66. In its application the Appellant requeste d that the Office appoints an expert: 
“In accordance with Article 78 (1) (e) and (3) of Re gulation (EC) No2100/94 and Article 61 of Regulation 
(EC) No 874/2009, and in particular from the third paragr aph of that Article, this party requests the Office 
to agree to issue an expert report by independent expert /s appointed by the Office so  that: (...) (b) It will  
be concluded that the mandarin orange variety, which is said to have been discovered at the INRA of 
Morocco in 1981/2 and which was registered by that inst itution as INRA W. 21 at the INRA station in Beni 
Mellal (also known as ‘W. Murcott’)  is, genetically and phenotypically, the same variety as the variety of 
mandarin that has been protected under the terms of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety, from plant material derived 
from INRA.CIRAD de San Giuliano in Corsica supplied by  Domaines Agricoles, and initially registered at its 
premises in Dar es Salam, such as ‘Murcott Sasma’, and subsequently renamed ‘Afourer’ at the request of 
Mr Nadori” (Application, p. 192 and footnote on pa ge 31 of the Statement of Grounds 21 April 2020).  
67. This request was dismissed by the Office which concluded that  
"For all this, and without considering now the burden of proof, proportionality, relevance and usefulness 
of the evidence that will be dealt la ter on in this document, it has been  proven that the expert report as 
such is not relevant to determine the distinctness of  the ‘Nadorcott’ variety." (contested decision, par. 
129). 
68. The Appellant submits that the dismissal of its reques t constitutes an “Infringement of the principle of 
sound administration and of the right to effective judi cial protection” as guaran teed by Article 41 (2) of 
the Charter of Fundamental Rights.  
69. The Board recalls that as a matter of principle it is the task of the judicial an d quasi-judicial Body to 
determine the usefulness and relevance of a piece of evid ence the filing of which is requested by a party. 
(9 June 2011, Cases C-465/09 P to C-470/09 EU:C:2011:372, § 108). 
70. The rejection of a request for the production of docu ments must be motivated and the (quasi-) Judicial 
Body must explain why the document is not material  to the outcome of the case (4 March 1999, C-119/97 
P, EU:C:1999:116, § 111).  
71. The Board considers the breeding history of ‘Nadorco tt’ extensively and suffici ently disclosed. For the 
granting of the Breeders’ Right only the informatio n as requested in the application form has to be 
provided. That information is important for the technical examination of the variety. It is up to the applicant 
to choose the material of the variety as delivered to the Office. It is not relevant whether that material 
could be similar to other selections of the applicant or selections from other sources. The only important characteristic in this regard is the distinctness of th e variety in comparison to all other varieties of common 
knowledge at the time of the application. This has in  extenso been tested by the Office with a positive 
result.  
72. In the present case, the Office concluded in the Ob servations in reply, §§§ 53, 59, and 61, that the 
Appellant’s request failed to meet the requirements of relevance, utility and proportionality, with a 
reference to the decision of the Genera l Court in case T-177/16 (Mema), § 44. 

  
 8 / 18
73. The Board observes that the Appellant could have commissioned its own expert report.  
74. The Appellant alleges that “Euroseeds neither had nor had access to INRA tree W21 existing at the INRA 
station in Beni Mellal, nor does it have access to the ‘Nadorcott’ trees of the parc el of the IVIA on which 
the [DUS] test was carried out” (pag e 31 of its Statement of grounds).  
75. As outlined by the Office in its observations filed in  the course of the present appeal the Appellant had 
access to the INRA W 21 tree and the ‘Nadorcott’ tree (§ 64 of the Office Observations).  
76. Article 78 BR and Articles 60 and 61 PR provide that the Office may hear an expert. Nothing requires the 
Office to appoint an expert at the request of a party to the proceedings.  
77. Furthermore, the request to appoint an expert mu st satisfy the principle of proportionality.  
78. The Board concurs with the Office, as stated in para graph 63 of its Observations, that the expert report 
would be redundant and therefore irrelevant. 
79. Moreover, a technical report would, in the case of ma ndarins, take six years at least for growing the trees 
and collecting the observations. Another issue is the coll ection of the original material of the selections as 
mentioned by the Appellant, which may, af ter so many years, appear impossible. 
2.6  The dismissal of Appellant’s request that the Office orders the production of documents  
80. The Appellant requested the Office to order the production of a number of documents. 
81. The Office rejected the request considering “that th e request for Euroseeds covers a demeasured amount 
of documentation, which is also viti ated by a lack of precision. Thus, the applicant’s wording is too vague 
(‘all information and documents’) and the scope of the evidence is dispro portionate to the importance that 
such information and documents may have for the purp ose of the investigation.” (Contested decision, § 
225).  
82. Furthermore, the Office expressed the concern that th e documents were in the possession of third parties 
located in Morocco which would delay the conduct of the proceedings (Contested decision, § 225).  
83. The Board concludes that also this request amounts to  nothing more than a "fishing expedition". Indeed, 
the Appellant failed to indicate with sufficient precis ion the documents that should be filed in the course 
of the proceedings and their relevance.  
84. The Board recalls again that as a matter of principle it is  the task of the judicial and quasi-judicial Body to 
determine the usefulness and relevance of a piece of evid ence the filing of which is requested by a party. 
(9 June 2011, Cases C-465/09 P to C-470/09 EU:C:2011:372, § 108). 
85. The Appellant finally argues that the Office breach ed its right to be heard since it decided on the 
admissibility of its request without providing the Appellant with the opportunity to be heard. 
86. The Board recalls that the right to be heard covers a ll the factual and legal evid ence which forms the basis 
for the decision-making act but not the final positi on which the administration intends to adopt (3 
December 2003, T-16/02, TD I, EU:T:2003:327, § 75).   
87. Furthermore, it should be noted that in 2016, in the course of the proceedings the owner and the interested 
party objected to the request. In its submissions of 2017, the Appellant responded to the allegations 
(Appellant submissions 2017, pp.47-75). 
2.7 Conclusion regarding the first ground of appeal 
88. During the nullity proceedings, the Office had numero us pieces of evidence at its disposal, from the 
application procedure, from FECOAV’s appeal and from the numerous arguments and documents provided 
by the parties to the nullity proceedings.  
89. Those facts and evidence were examined carefully an d impartially by the Office and were considered 
sufficient to be able to take, with all the guaran tees, the decision refusing the nullity petition.  
90. The Office did not consider necessary or appropriat e to prolong the nullity proceedings further, with 
additional inquiries into aspects on which it had no doubt that it needed  clarification, also for reasons of 
sound administration and legal certainty.  

  
 9 / 18
91. Such a conclusion also falls within  the broad discretion enjoyed by th e Office as regards the nullity of 
CPVRs as recognised by the Court of Justice (21 May 2015, C-546/12 P, Ralf Schräder vs CPVO, 
EU:C:2015:332, § 56).  
92. The first ground of appeal is therefore rejected.  
 
3. Second ground of appeal: the Nadorcott variety was discovered and not developed  
93. The Appellant argues that the 'Nadorcott' variety wa s discovered without further development thereafter 
so that it would not have been the subject of ‘b reeding’ and therefore not subject to protection. 
94. The burden of proof that the ‘Nadorcott’ variety was di scovered and not developed lies with the Appellant.  
95. Article 1 of the 1991 UPOV Convention  provides that “(iv) “breeder” means - the person who bred, or 
discovered and developed, a variety”. The use of the wo rd “or” clearly indicates that a new and protectable 
variety can be the result of breeding or of a discovery and development. 
96. The notion of “discovery” describes the activity of “s election within natural vari ation” while “development” 
describes the process of “propagation and evaluati on” of the variety discovered (UPOV Document C 
(Extr.)/19/2/, point 16, the “2002 Document”).  
97. The Appellant argues that the Office makes an inte rpretation of the concept of development which is 
contrary to the interpretation given by UPOV.  
98. To this end, the Appellant undertakes a detailed st udy of the work on the elaboration of the 2002 
Document and the Explanatory Notes on the defini tion of breeder under the 1991 Act of the UPOV 
Convention, Document UPOV/EXN/BRD of 2013.  
99. The Appellant argues that the UPOV explanator y notes Document UPOV/EXN/BRD/1 (the “2013 
Document”), has changed the position  of UPOV on the concept of disc overy and development previously 
set out in the “2002 Document” and excludes from pr otection those varieties discovered which were 
subject to “unchanged” multiplication.  
100. The Board does not agree. First, the amendments made in 2013 are irrelevant since the contested 
application for registration of Comm unity plant variety rights for the va riety ‘Nadorcott’ of the botanical 
taxon Citrus reticulata Blanco (Applic ation  number 1995/0726) was filed in 1995. 
101. For the sake of completeness,  the Board notes that  while it is true that the 2013 Document added the 
phrase “a person shall not be entitled to obtain th e protection of an existing variety that has been 
discovered and reproduced unchanged” , the purpose of that addition is to avoid the protection of mere 
findings made by people who discover a variety alre ady cultivated in nature or a population of plant 
material that has already been reproduced and is be ing cultivated, without performing any selection or 
evaluation work, limiting the applic ant to reproduce it without change, precisely because this does not 
meet the ultimate purpose pursued by the UPOV, which is to contribute to the development of agriculture 
with the creation and disseminati on of new improved varieties. 
102. The Board is of the view that, as mentioned in th e article of Mr. Nadori, referred to in paragraph 145 
below, the ‘Nadorcott’ variety was subject to breedin g and development, including crossing, selection, 
multiplication and evaluation  of the variety. The variety originates from seedlings grown from seeds of the 
variety ‘Murcott’. The “discovery” of the variety in 1981-82 by Mr. Nadori is an act of selection in a 
population created by the breeder.  
103. After becoming aware in 1988 of its self-incompatibilit y, the existence of which the Appellant does not 
dispute, the ats performed by Mr. Nadori can be qualifie d as acts of development of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety. 
The experimentation initiated and conducted by Mr Na dori led to the development of a set of processes 
necessary to obtain a fruit without seeds. 
104. The second ground of appeal is therefore rejected. 
 
 
 

  
 10 / 18 
4. Third ground of appeal: lack of legal capacity of Mr Nadori to assign the rights pertaining to 
the ‘Nadorcott’ variety to Mr De Maistre 
105. The Appellant essentially submits that under the la ws of the Kingdom of Morocco Mr Nadori was not 
entitled to assign the rights pertaining to the ‘Nador cott’ variety since the rights belong to its employer.  
4.1 Admissibility of the opinion of the Morocca n law firm Bennani & Associés LLP (Annex 1) 
106. In the course of the present appeal proceedings the Appellant filed an opinion of the Moroccan law firm 
Bennani & Associés LLP (Annex 1) which concludes on the basis of the Law but also the practice of INRA that the rights developed by an employee belong to the employer. 
107. The obligation to provide facts and evidence togeth er with the application for annulment derives from 
Article 53a (2) PR, which provides that “An application to  the Office to initiate the proceedings for invalidity 
or annulment pursuant to Articles 20 and 21 respectively of the basic regulation shall be accompanied by 
evidence and facts which raise serious doubts as to th e validity of the title and shall contain the following 
elements: (...) (b) (ii) an indication of the facts, evidence and arguments presented in support of these 
grounds”.  
108. This provision is supplemented by Art. 76 BR which st ates that “It shall not take into account facts or 
evidence submitted outside the time limit set by the Office”.  
109. Consequently, it follows from the wording of those prov isions that the CPVO cannot take into account facts 
and evidence that are submitted out of time (24 September 2019, T ‑112/18 Pink Lady par. 97). 
110. Moreover, for the sake of completeness, the Board note s that the Appellant did no t provide any explanation 
why said evidence was not transmitted in the course of the Invalidity Proceedings.  
111. Consequently, the Board concludes that the evidence submitted for the first time in the course of the 
appeal proceedings is inadmissible.  
112. But even if said evidence would be admissible this would not change the outcome of the present appeal.  
4.2. Lack of standing of the Ap pellant to contest the ownership  
113. Article 20(1)(c) BR provides that a variety shall be decl ared invalid if it is established that the right has 
been granted to a person who is not entitled to it, unle ss it is transferred to the pe rson who is so entitled.  
114. The Appellant does not claim to be the legitimate owne r of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety. As a result, it has no 
standing to contest the validity of said variety on the basis of a lack of ownership or entitlement.  
4.3. The issue of ownership 
115. The opinion of Bennani & Associés confirms that (i) at  the time of the discover y and development of the 
‘Nadorcott’ variety Moroccan Law did not contain any ru le defining the ownership of a variety developed 
by an employee. There was no Moroccan rule on empl oyment contracts defining to whom the right to 
protection corresponded; (ii) the rules adopted subs equently in 2000 are not retroactive; and (iii) the 
agreement between employer and employee is perfectl y valid and admissible and does not have to be 
made in writing. 
116. The Board also notes that the Moroccan Law of 1916 esta blishes that the right belonged to the author of 
the discovery, in the present case Mr Nadori and that it has not been proven that  the internal policy or 
private contracts with the employees of INRA (Mr Na dori’s employer) excluded Mr. Nadori from holding 
ownership. In fact, INRA has never disputed  Mr. Nadori’s entitlement to the right. 
117. 
Therefore, even if the right had been vested in INRA  or Domaines Agricoles, no specific formality was 
necessary to execute the assignment.  
118. Furthermore, it is important to note that INRA or Domaines Agricoles never questioned the ownership of 
Mr Nadori or the subsequent assignment. Right to the contrary both entities have recognised Mr Nadori’s 
right and its subsequent transfer to NCP. Furthermore,  both entities are shareholders of NCP and benefit 
from the exploitation of ‘Nadorcott’ in this way. 
119. Finally, the Board fully endorses th e conclusion of the contested decisi on (par.158): “In this regard, the 
conduct of the Moroccan INRA itself, which has not been challenged in any way [during] the time when 

  
 11 / 18 
the right to obtain entitlement belonged to Mr Nado ri reinforces the above co nclusion. In fact, in the 
statement made by the Director General of INRA, Mr Mohamed Badraoui on 9 December 2016 and attached as Document 9 to your letter of 2016, [it is  confirmed] that the Commi ssion [...] consented both 
to the adoption of conservation measures for the protec tion of the variety and to validity of the subsequent 
transfers to Mr De Maistre and subsequently to NCP. 
120. For the sake of completeness, by letter of 3 Decemb er 1982, Mr Nadori informed Prof. Bitters that the 
INRA had accepted the sending of your new selections, which again seems to confirm not only that INRA […] was aware of Mr Nadori’s activities, but especially  [their consent to him].  Even if the Moroccan law 
of the time had provided that the du ty on the acquisition belonged to th e employer, in this case the INRA, 
it seems that it was not only recogn ising the right to be obtained by Mr  Nadori, but also consented to his 
subsequent assignments to third parties.” 
121. The third ground of appeal is therefore rejected.  
 
5. Fourth ground of appeal: lack of entitlement to file an applicatio n for a Community plant 
variety right since the developer is citizen of the Kingdom of Morocco 
122. The Appellant submits that since the Kingdom of Morocco was not member of UPOV, Mr. Nadori, a 
Moroccan citizen, was not legally entitl ed to file an application for the pr otection of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety 
in the European Community. 
123. Article 12 BR as in force in 1994 provided for:  
Entitlement to file an application fo r a Community plant variety right   
An application for a Community plant variety right may be filed by any natural or legal person, or any body 
ranking as a legal person under the law applicable to th at body, provided they are: (a) nationals of one of 
the Member States or nationals of a member of the Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants 
within the meaning of Article 1 (xi)  of the Act of 1991 of the Internatio nal Convention for the Protection 
of New Varieties of Plants, or are domiciled or have their seat or an establishment in such a State;(…). 
124. The application was filed by Mr De Maistre who, as a French citizen, was undoubtedly entitled to file the 
application.  
125. The Board further endorses the conclusion of the cont ested decision that one should not confuse the issue 
of entitlement to the protection (Article 11 BR) and th e entitlement to file the application (Article 12 BR). 
126. The entitlement to Community protection is substantiv e in nature and is regulated in Article 11 BR. This 
status is held by anyone who breeds, or discovers and develops, a variety, as well as by his successors in 
title. Article 11 BR does not esta blish any nationality requirement. 
127. On the other hand, the entitlement to file applicat ions under Article 12 BR is a procedural right, 
independent of the right to variety protection, and re lates only to the question  who can be an applicant 
for a variety right; this applicant does not necessarily have to be the breeder. 
128. The BR does not require the breeder and the applicant to be the same person; nor does it state that the 
breeder must have the capacity to file the application in  order for an application filed by an assignee of its 
rights (with capacity) to be valid. In fact, Re gulation No. 15/2008, which removed the nationality 
requirement for the entitlement to file applications, spec ifies in its Recital 2, that this reform was intended 
to simplify the procedure, and that one application system for all applicants should be introduced.  
129. The fourth ground of appeal is therefore rejected.  
 
6. Fifth, sixth and eighth ground of appeal: Lack of  Novelty as the result of certain transfers of 
plant material of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety 
6.1. The issue of novelty 
130. Article 10 BR provides that:  
“1. A variety shall be deemed to be new if, at the da te of application determined pursuant to Article 51, 
variety constituents or harvested material of the vari ety have not been sold or otherwise disposed of to 

  
 12 / 18 
others, by or with the consent of th e breeder within the meaning of Articl e 11, for purposes of exploitation 
of the variety: 
(a) earlier than one year before the abovemention ed date, within the territory of the Community; 
(b) earlier than four years or, in the case of trees or of vines, earlier than six years before the said date, 
outside the territory of the Community. 
2. The disposal of variety constituents  to an official body for statutory purposes, or to others on the basis 
of a contractual or other legal relationship solely fo r production, reproduction, mu ltiplication, conditioning 
or storage, shall not be deemed to be a disposal to  others within the meaning of paragraph 1, provided 
that the breeder preserves the exclusive right of disp osal of these and other variety constituents, and no 
further disposal is made. […]  
3. The disposal of variety constituents or harvested material of the variety, which have been produced 
from plants grown for the purposes specified in Arti cle 15 (b) and (c) and which are not used for further 
reproduction or multiplication, shall not be deemed to  be exploitation of the variety, unless reference is 
made to the variety for purpos es of that disposal […].” 
However, where an application for a CPVR has been file d within one year from the entry into force of the 
Basic Regulation (1 September 1994) a derogation exis ts by virtue of Article 116(1) BR which states: 
“Notwithstanding Article 10 (1) (a) and without prejud ice to the provisions of Article 10 (2) and (3), a 
variety shall be deemed to be new also in cases wher e variety constituents or harvested material thereof 
have not been sold or otherwise disposed of to others , by or with the consent of  the breeder, within the 
territory of the Community for purposes  of exploitation of the variety, earlier than four years, in the case 
of trees or of vines earlier than six years, before th e entry into force of this Regulation, if the date of 
application is within one year of that date.” 
131. Taking into account that Regulation No. 2100/94 entered into force on 1 September 1994, and the 
application date for CPVR for 'Nador cott' being 22 August 1995, Article 116 (1) BR is applicable in this 
case. 
132. As far as the burden of proof is concerned, the broad discretion of the Office also applies to the novelty 
requirement which includes that a third party requesti ng the nullity of a CPVR because of lack of novelty 
has to provide substantive evidence raising serious do ubts as to the validity of the title (21 May 2015, C-
546/12P, Lemon Symphony II, EU:C:2015:332, §§ 56-57).  
133. This conclusion, is confirmed by th e introduction of Article 53a PR st ating that when a nullity request is 
initiated by a third party's request, it shall be acco mpanied by evidence and fa cts raising serious doubts 
as to the validity of the title. 
6.2. The alleged commercial exploitation in 1966 of  components of the ‘Afourer’ variety in Morocco 
carried out before the intended discovery of Mr Nadori at INRA’s premises 
134. In support of his position, the A ppellant submitted a notary document including the statement of Mr 
Martínez Úbeda, manager of a 80 hectare citrus farm in Beni Mellal, owned by Mr Cheran, which testified to the purchase of the plants of ‘Afourer’ and their pl anting on that estate in 1966, further forwarding in 
1968 of the harvested fruit of these mandarins, which matured in February-March, to the packaging plant 
in Casablanca, for marketing in Fr ance, via a company owned by Mr Kerrer, known as Transport Chez 
Vineyard. 
135. The Board considers that a statement made fifty ye ars after the facts is not reliable. Indeed, the 
imperfection of memory is a well-known fact. Memory distortions occur simply with the passage of time.  
136. Moreover, if the ‘Nadorcott’ variety would have b een exploited in the 60’s, this would have been 
documented (nurseries, points of sale, invoices, transpor t etc.). This is not the case here. Additionally, the 
original ‘Nadorcott’ tree was identified 16 years later in 1982 and encoded as INRA 21 W. 
6.3. Transfer to Mr Bitters (professor at th e University of California, Riverside (UCR) 
a. The transfer  
137. The supply of said material to Mr Bitters of the UCR is not disputed (Contested decision, par. 186). 

  
 13 / 18 
b. The purpose of the transfer 
138. It appears also from the letters exchanged between Mr  Nadori and Professor Bitters that the material was 
provided for “investigation purposes ”: In the letter sent by Prof Bitters to Mr Nadori, on 5 October 1982 
Prof Bitters writes: “would you share these cultivars wi th our research center at Riverdale?” (doc. 65). In 
return, Professor Bitters offered Mr Nadori citrus  plants for the INRA citrus variety collection. 
139. The correspondence exchanged thereafter on 3 De cember 1982 (document 66 of the Application for 
nullity) and 22 December 1982 (documents 67 and 68 of the Application for nullity) confirm that 
conclusion.  
140. The correspondence exchanged between Mr Bitters and Mr  Nadori does not contain a single reference to 
a commercial exploitation of the variety.  
141. The Appellant argues that the sole fact that the plant is not sold does not mean that there is no commercial 
exploitation of the plant variety (SOG , page 8). To support that statemen t the Appellant refers to a German 
Publication: Leßmann, Herbert, Würtenberger, Deut sches und europäisches Sortenschutzrecht, Handbuch, 
2nd ed., at paragraph 136: “The supply of new material to another breeder, in the expectation that it will 
also deliver material to the donor for new varieties, is intended to promote the activity as the breeder. 
Therefore, it has a commercial purpose or with the purpose of opening the use of the variety by the 
recipient. “ 
142. This extract of the publication is cited out of contex t. “Therefore it has a co mmercial purpose (…) opening 
the use of the variety” is an incorrect reasoning. There is a habit between breeders to exchange samples 
of their new varieties/selections for observations, trialing and even for breeding under the breeder’s 
exemption. 
It is an unofficial code that this material  is not being used for commercial purposes.  
In the case of ‘Nadorcott’ it is cl ear that the Moroccan INRA in 1988 had the attitude to share its knowledge 
and material with other interested in stitutions for scientific purposes. 
UCR profited eventually from the ‘Nadorcott’ material by developing ‘Tang Gold’ as an improvement of the 
important characteristic ‘seedless mandarins”. 
143. Furthermore, this interpretation co nflicts with the conclusion reache d by the General Court in Case T-
112/18 (‘Cripps Pink’), § 68: “It follows from this case-l aw that the concept of ‘exploitation’ of the variety 
within the meaning of Article 10(1) of the Basic Regulation relates to exploitation for profit, as further 
demonstrated by the provisions of th e Basic Regulation relating to contra ctual exploitation rights, but this 
concept excludes commercial trials aimed at assessing varieties under commercial conditions across a range of soil types and different farming systems to  determine their value to customers (24 September 
2019, T-112/18, Cripps Pink, EU:T:2019:679, par. 68).  
144. The Appellant also argues that assignments for a pr imary purpose of investigation, in which commercial 
exploitation is not excluded must be interpreted as allowing the commercial exploi tation. In support of its 
assertion the Appellant refers to Article 10(2) BR. 
145. This position is not supported by the case law. 
146. The absence of an express prohibition of commercia l use does not make the delivery for commercial 
purposes: 
§34 The purpose of [Article 10(2) BR] is therefore to  specify the circumstances in which certain legal 
situations are or are not covered by the concept of di sposal for purposes of exploitation of the variety 
within the meaning of Article 10(1) of the Basic Regulation. Contrary to what the applicant appears to 
claim, conditions that must be met in order for a disp osal to be considered not to negate novelty are not 
cumulative. [...] 
§58 Moreover, as already set out in paragraph 34 above,  since the function of Article 10(2) of the Basic 
Regulation is merely to specify the conditions under which certain types of disposal do not constitute a 
disposal ‘to a third party’, the applicant cannot claim th at the conditions in this provision must be fulfilled 
in all cases in order for a sale or disposal not to nega te novelty. Indeed, for a sale or disposal made outside 
the European Union before the grace period not to negate novelty, it is sufficient under Article 10(1) of 
the Basic Regulation that the sale or disposal to a third party is not made by the breeder or with his 

  
 14 / 18 
consent ‘for purposes of exploitation of the variety’ , which is a separate condition from those set out in 
Article 10(2) of that regulation.” (24-09-2019, T-112/18, Pink Lady §§34, 58 (underlining Board of Appeal). 
Moreover, during the hearing on 3 October 2022, a furthe r question to the Appellant was to know if there 
is any evidence of commercialisation  of the variety by UCR in 1984-85, before the discovery of the seedless 
characteristic in 1988. Nothing in the exchange of  letters (evidence) shows that there was a clear 
commercial interest in the variety for the California n growers or a clear commercialisation of the variety 
at that time.  
Although the Appellant replied that there was a commerc ial interest for the variety as from the date the 
material was received by UCR in 1985 but the whole pr ocess applied by UCR took some years before the 
variety could be distributed and comme rcialised, the Appellant had no ha rd evidence of commercialisation 
prior to the grace period. 
147. In light with the evidence prov ided by the parties, the Board concludes that the transfer of 
INRA21W/Nadorcott material by Mr Nadori to Prof Bitte rs, does not amount to commercial exploitation of 
the variety in question before the relevant date.  
6.4.  The disposal of material to the SASMA nurseries in 1982 
a.  The transfer 
148. The transfer of plant material obtained from the IN RA21W tree to the Société des Services Agricoles au 
Maroc (‘SASMA’) is 1982 is not contested.  
b.  The purpose of the transfer 
149. In an  article ‘Nadorcott: une nouvelle variété prometteus e’ (Exhibit 6 of the Nullity Application), Mr  Nadori 
explains that plant material obtained from the INRA21W  tree was sent to the Société des Services Agricoles 
au Maroc (‘SASMA’) to conduct trials in experimental plots in various regions of Morocco (‘Des greffons de 
l’arbre INRA21W ont été remis à la Société des Serv ices Agricoles au Maroc (SASMA) en 1982-83 sous le 
code INRA-W en vue d’essais de comportement […] Des parcelles de comportement de 2 à 4 ha de la sélection INRA-W ont été installées, ch ez des agriculteurs, par la SASMA’). 
150. The same statement is found in US Patent 10,480 rela ting to ‘Nadorcott’ (Exhibit 41 of the Nullity 
Application), which indicates that ‘Budwood of ‘Inr a W’ was asexually propagated on a small scale for 
experimental purposes at the Sasma Nursery in Menasr a at the region of Kenitra. Later and in early 1983, 
experimental plots of 2 to 4 hectares were also established […]’. 
6.5. In the article ‘Nadorcott mandarin: a new promising variety’ (Exhibit 5 of the Nullity 
Application) Prof. Nadori indicated that the plantings carried out in 1982-1983 were for ‘behaviour trials’. 
151. The article by Malika Alami ‘Objectif Excellence’, pu blished in l’Écho, No 208 (Exhibit 8 of the Nullity 
Application), indicates the plantings were to test the variety (‘qui également testaient le produit’). 
152. The ‘Informational note regarding the ‘Nadorcott’ variety of ‘Group I - Tangerines’ of the Spanish Plant 
Varieties Office (Exhibit 27 of the Nullity Application) also states th at ‘During 1983 to 1985 it was tested 
in experimental plots’. 
153. With regard to the disposal of mate rial to SASMA nurseries at the begi nning of the 80s, th e Board, on the 
basis of the evidence described above, concludes that it  falls within the concept of  acts of development of 
the variety, such as testing trials on experimental plots.  
154. The disposal for the purposes of testing of the variety which does not amount to sale or disposal to third 
parties for purposes of exploitation of the variety do es not negate novelty for the purposes of Article 10 
of the Basic Regulation (11/04/2019, Kiku v CPVO — Sächsisches Landesamt für Umwelt, Landwirtschaft 
und Geologie (Pinova), T ‑765/17).  
155. It follows from the case-law that the concept of ‘exp loitation’ of the variety within the meaning of Article 
10(1) BR  relates to exploitation for profit, as fu rther demonstrated by the provisions of the Basic 
Regulation relating to contractual exploitation righ ts, and that this concept excludes commercial trials 
aimed at assessing varieties under commercial conditions  across a range of soil types and different farming 

  
 15 / 18 
systems to determine their value to  customers (24 September 2019, T ‑112/18, Cripps Pink, 
EU:T:2019:679, par. 68). 
6.6. The transfer of plant material made by Do maines Agricoles to the Spanish producer Rafael 
Boronat Flores in 1988  
a.  The transfer 
156. In its application for a declaration of invalidity (see pp. 163 and 87), the Appellant alleges that propagation 
material was further transferred by Mr Boronat to various Spanish producers for commercial purposes in 
1988.  
157. Mr. Boronat merely states that he brought material “in 1988” “in a suitcase”. The statement lacks precision 
and is inconsistent. 
158. Furthermore, the witness failed to provide the relevant documentation (Regulatory authorisation, Customs 
documentation, Quarantine) that would confirm the export to Spain.  
159. If true, the material was irregularly obta ined, without the consent of the breeder. 
160. Moreover, the date is unspecified (allegedly “in 1988”). 
161. Prof. Nadori informed the director of Domaines Agrico les of the results of his investigation in September 
1989 (doc. 6 nullity application). Domaines Agricoles could not have authorized any disposal of plant 
material of ‘Nadorcott’ to third parties. 
162. INIA’s report about the age of Mr. Boronat’s tree is  inconclusive (“we cannot assure 100%”) and states 
that it would date from 1985, which is inconsistent  with Boronat’s account of facts and suggests the 
existence of over-grafting. 
163. The remaining documents cited by the A ppellant also rebut this allegation. 
164. According to the statement of the IVIA former Director  (doc. 48 nullity application) the IVIA does not have 
a record of the date of introducti on or the age of Boronat’s tree.  
165. According to the statement of the Spanish Secretary of Agriculture (doc. 52 nullity application) the first 
disposal of ‘Nadorcott’ in Spain was in 1996 (and unconsented). 
166. These transfers are not disputed by  the parties, nor by the Office. 
b. Timing of the transfer 
167. The Office concludes that these transfers do not destro y the novelty of the plant variety since they occurred 
after the start of the grace period (i.e. 1 Se ptember 1988) (contested decision par. 208).   
168. This conclusion is not contested.  
6.7. The subsequent commercial use by CCPP since 1993 
169. The fact that said material was, wi thin the grace period, eventually used  in the United States by CCPP for 
commercial purposes is not disputed. 
170. However there is no proof on file that Mr Nadori or any other person consented to such transfer and 
commercial use.  
171. In any event, as already explained, the conditions of  Article 10 BR are not cumulative (24 September 2019, 
T‑112/18, Pink, EU:T:2019:679, par. 34).  
 7. Seventh ground of appeal: application of the Grace Period provided for in Article 116 BR  
172. The Appellant essentially claims that since the Applican t did not file a valid applic ation it could not benefit 
from the Grace Period.  
173. In the present case it is not disputed that the Appl ication was filed one week before the expiry of the 
period of one year following the entry into force of the Regulation, as required by Article 116 BR.  

  
 16 / 18 
174. However, the Appellant submits that the application do es not comply with articles 50 and 51 BR since it 
contains manifest errors in relation to the name of the breeder, to the technical description of the variety 
and to any previous commercialization of the variety (SOG, par. 273).   
175. The misleading character of informat ion transmitted to a public author ity constitutes an abuse of right 
(see by way of analogy 1 July 2010, T-321/ 05, EU: T: 2010: 266, paragraphs 355-356). 
176. As a result, the Appellant submits that the filing of  the application mentioned would be null and void. 
177. Article 51 BR reads as follows:  
The date of application for a Communi ty plant variety right shall be the date on which a valid application 
was received by the Office pursuant to Article 49 (1) (a) or by a sub-office or national agency pursuant to Article 49 (1) (b), provided it complies with Article 50 (1) and subject to payment of the fees due pursuant 
to Article 83 within a time limit specified by the Office. 
178. Article 50 BR provides a list of the informatio n that must be included in an application: 
1. The application for a Communi ty plant variety right must co ntain at least the following: 
(a) […] (b) […] (c) […] (d) the name of the breeder and an assurance that, to  the best of the applicants knowledge, no further 
persons have been involved in the breeding, or discov ery and development, of the variety; if the applicant 
is not the breeder, or not the only breeder, he shall provide the relevant documentary evidence as to how 
the entitlement to the Community plant variety right came into his possession; 
(e) […] (f) a technical description of the variety; (g) […]  (h) […]  (i) details of any previous commercialization of the variety; (j) details of any other application made in respect of the variety.  
7.1. The alleged error with regard to the identity of the Breeder 
179. The  application for protection and the technical questi onnaire (Exhibit no 10) indicates Mr Nadori as the 
breeder of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety.  
180. The Appellant contests that statement on the basis of  an article entitled ‘La Mandarine Nadorcott: Une 
nouvelle variete prometteuse’ published in Revue HT No 132 — Sep./Dec. 2005, pp. 23 et seq, where Mr 
Nadori indicates that three persons were actually invo lved in the development of the variety, namely Mr 
Nadori El Bachir, Ouammou Mohamed and Ouaïcha Rahhou. 
181. Subsequently, on 1 December 2016, Mr Nadori rectified its previous statement and declared that he was 
the sole breeder while Mr Ouammou Mohamed and Mr Ouaïcha Rahhou were two technicians involved in the performance of tasks such as maintenance of the estates, collection of samples, measures, weight of 
fruits, analysis, etc (see Exhibits 9 and 11 of NCP’s response to the Nullity Application). 
182. In the course of the invalidity proc eedings, the Appellant argued that li ttle weight should be given to the 
statement made by Mr Nadori, since he is an interested party.  
183. It is true that in order to assess the evidential value of a document, regard should be had first and foremost 
to the credibility of the account it contains. It is ne cessary to take account, in particular, of the person 
from whom the document originates, the circumstances in which it came into being, the person to whom 
it was addressed and whether, on its face, the docu ment appears sound and reliable (7 June 2005, T-
303/03, Salvita, EU:T:2005:200, § 42). 

  
 17 / 18 
184. Since the declaration stems from a person who has a personal interest in the outcome of the case and 
moreover, is employed by, the party concerned, it is of less probativ e value than documents which emerge 
from third parties. (see for example 17 March 2016, C-252/15 P, SMART WATER, EU:C:2016:178, § 61; 
25 October 2013, T-416/11, Cardio manager, EU:T: 2013:559, § 41; 11 December 2014, T-196/13, la nana 
(fig.), EU:T:2014:674, § 32). 
185. However, the Board notes that the amended statement of Mr Nadori was never contested by Mr Ouammou 
Mohamed and Mr Ouaïcha Rahhou.  
186. At the hearing the Appellant confirmed that it ne ver tried to contact Mr Ouammou Mohamed and Mr 
Ouaïcha Rahhou to discuss their resp ective involvement in the developm ent of the impugned variety.   
7.2.  The failure to disclose the information relating to the disposal of the plant variety and of the 
fruit of its harvest prior to the date of its submission 
187. In the application for a Community plant variety right relating to the variety ‘Nadorcott’ Mr De Maistre 
stated that the fruits of the variety had been market ed since January 1994 in France and in other countries 
under the brand name ‘Afourer’. He also made reference to sales of fruit from January 1994 in Abu Dhabi, also under the brand name ‘Afourer’. 
188. The Appellant submits that the applicant fa iled to disclose the following facts: 
-  The sale to Prof. Bitters and UCR in the years 1984-85 of plant material of the variety; 
- The sale to various producers in Morocco in cluding to Domaines Agricoles in 1982;  
- The marketing of the product of the harvest of the ‘Nadorcott’ variety before applying for protection 
in Morocco and at least by the end of 1993 or the beginning of 1994 in the European Union; 
- The transfer of plant material made by Domaines Agricoles to the Spanish producer Rafael Boronat  
Flores in 1988. 
189. These arguments have all been addressed in the preceding sections.  
7.3. The failure to disclose certain technical information  
190. The Appellant argues that the applicant failed to pr ovide certain technical information on the development 
of the plant variety.  
191. However, the Board concurs with the Office that  ”n either Article 50 BR nor Article 19 PR requires the 
applicant for protection to describe how the discovery and development of the variety took place, but that 
the maximum required is that the lo cality in which the variety was obta ined or discovered and developed 
and information on the maintenance and spread of the variety or the characteristics (Article 19.2 (e) PR) 
should be indicated as relevant.” 
192. It appears that the technical questionnaire for ‘Nadorcott’ satisfies these requirements. 
193. The seventh ground of appeal is therefore rejected.  
 8.  Eighth ground of appeal:  the sale in France and other countries of the European Union of 
fruit from the ‘Nadorcott’ mandarin variety before the period of grace. 
 
194. In its application for invalidity (pp. 94 et seq.), the Appellant alleged that  fruits from trees of the ‘Afourer’ 
variety (‘Nadorcott’) coming from Do maines Agricoles in Morocco were exported and marketed in several 
countries of the European Union at the end of 1993 and the beginning of 1994. This use would have 
started before the grace period as provided for in Article 10 BR and the  transitional regime provided 
for in Article 116. 
This issue has already been discussed in relation to th e seventh ground of appeal, with the conclusion that 
alleged sales took place during the grace period. 
195. 
The eighth ground of appeal is therefore rejected. 
 

  
 18 / 18 
 
 9.  Ninth ground of appeal: identity of varieties 196. The Appellant submits that there is no proof that the tree of mandarin INRA W 21 discovered in 1980/81 
at INRA’s premises in Beni Mellal, and the trees iden tified as Murcott Sasma planted in Domaines Agricoles 
in Dar es Salam, belong to one and the same  variety and not to two distinct varieties. 
197. As stated here above in paragraph 65, the issue whether ‘INRA W21’ and ‘Murcott Sasma’ trees are 
identical or not is irrelevant. From this material only one distinct vari ety has been released and applied for 
CPVR: ‘Nadorcott’. 
198. The ninth ground of appeal is therefore rejected. 
 
III.    ON THE COSTS 
199. For the reasons set out above, the Board finds that the Appeal herein is not well-founded and decision 
No. NN 20 is upheld.  
200. As the losing party to an Appeal bears th e costs thereof, pursuant to Article 85(1)  of Council Regulation         
2100/94, the costs shall be borne by the Appellant.  
 
IV.    ON LEGAL REMEDIES  
201. Pursuant to Article 73 of the basic regulation an acti on may be brought before the Court of Justice against 
this decision within two months of its service.
